title: Acmella buphthalmoides
intro: Acmella buphthalmoides, also known as paracress or toothache plant, is a tropical plant species native to Central and South America, characterized by its small, white flowers and edible leaves. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which can help alleviate pain and reduce swelling, making it a popular ingredient in traditional medicine. The bioactive constituents of Acmella buphthalmoides include sesquiterpene lactones, particularly spilanthol, which is responsible for its numbing and anti-inflammatory effects. Acmella buphthalmoides is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, capsules, and creams, where it is often combined with other herbs to enhance its medicinal properties and provide relief from toothaches, arthritis, and other inflammatory conditions.
taxonomy: Acmella buphthalmoides is a species of flowering plant in the daisy family, Asteraceae. It is native to tropical and subtropical regions of Central and South America. This plant is classified in the genus Acmella, which includes other species of flowering plants commonly known as toothache plants or bitter leaf plants. Acmella buphthalmoides is further classified as a member of the subfamily Mutisioideae. The exact classification and relationships of Acmella buphthalmoides are part of ongoing research and study in the field of botany.
uses: Acmella buphthalmoides has been traditionally used in various folk remedies. Its medicinal properties include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. The plant has been used to treat wounds, skin conditions, and infections. In some cultures, Acmella buphthalmoides is used as a natural remedy for fever reduction and as an antiseptic agent. Further research is needed to confirm its efficacy and potential applications in modern medicine.
